Name: curvature-aware_precision_routing
Title: Curvature-Aware Precision Routing: Training-Free Optimization Proxies for Efficient Neural Network Inference
Short Hypothesis: A curvature-aware precision routing framework that leverages deterministic spectral estimators and formal condition number constraints can achieve efficient neural network inference without training, by dynamically allocating precision tiers based on geometric subspace diagnostics.
Related Work: Existing work on mixed-precision inference (e.g., Micikevicius et al., 2018) often relies on heuristic strategies or extensive training. Recent advances in training-free diagnostics (e.g., Gholami et al., 2021) focus on static precision allocation. Our proposal distinguishes itself by introducing a curvature-aware routing protocol that dynamically adjusts precision tiers based on formal geometric diagnostics, ensuring both efficiency and numerical stability.
Abstract: We propose a curvature-aware precision routing framework for efficient neural network inference. Unlike existing methods that rely on heuristic precision allocation or extensive training, our approach leverages deterministic spectral estimators and formal condition number constraints to dynamically allocate precision tiers based on geometric subspace diagnostics. Specifically, we deploy projection-based mechanistic probes—such as gradient covariance decay and Hessian conditioning—to quantify feature entanglement and isolate algorithmic pathologies. This enables a training-free routing protocol that replaces heuristic fragility claims with formalized stability radii and PAC-Bayes generalization bounds. Our framework enforces coarse-grained, block-level precision tiering to guarantee strict overhead bounds and preserve deterministic execution pathways. Empirical validation on standard benchmarks demonstrates that our approach achieves state-of-the-art efficiency-accuracy trade-offs, with explicit mathematical accounting for all numerical guardrails. By anchoring precision routing to architectural theory rather than systems engineering, we ensure resource efficiency and mathematical transparency.
Experiments: Evaluate the framework on CIFAR-10 and ImageNet datasets using ResNet and EfficientNet architectures.
Compare efficiency-accuracy trade-offs against state-of-the-art mixed-precision methods.
Quantify diagnostic overhead and numerical stability using formal condition number constraints.
Perform ablation studies to isolate the impact of curvature-aware routing components.
Validate generalization bounds using PAC-Bayes verification and multi-seed variance quantification.
Risk Factors and Limitations: The framework may introduce additional computational overhead due to diagnostic loops.
Effectiveness may vary across different architectures and datasets.
Formal condition number constraints may not fully capture all numerical instabilities.
The approach relies on accurate spectral estimators, which may be sensitive to noise.
